1: Psalm 141 opens with the urgency characteristic of the final Davidic cluster (Pss 138–145): "come quickly (ḥûšāh)!" — the same urgency-adverb found in Psalms 70:1 ("Hurry, O God, to save me!"). The double cry — "I call on you" and "hear my voice when I cry out to you" — is not mere repetition but the standard Psalter prayer-stance: the initial address and the request for admission. In the temple liturgy, a worshipper would approach the gate with a cry that required acknowledgment before entrance was granted (Psalms 24:3–5; Psalms 100:4).
2: "May my prayer rise before you like incense, and the lifting up of my hands like the evening offering" — tikkôn tĕpillātî qĕṭōret lĕpānêkā. The incense offering (qĕṭōret) was burned twice daily on the golden incense altar inside the tabernacle/temple (Exodus 30:7–8) — at the morning and evening tamid. The minḥāh (evening grain offering) was the "evening sacrifice" at 3pm. The speaker's prayer and lifted hands are identified with these daily offerings — turning individual prayer into a liturgical participation in the ongoing temple worship even when physically absent from the temple. Revelation 8:3–4 takes up this imagery for heavenly intercession: the golden censer, the incense, and "the prayers of all the saints" rising before God.
3: "Post a guard at my mouth, O LORD; station a sentry at the door of my lips" — the metaphor of civic fortification applied to speech: the mouth as a gate that requires a guard to control who or what passes through. In the ANE city, gates were heavily defended and carefully controlled — the gate determined who entered, what goods came through, and what justice was dispensed (Ruth 4:1–11; Amos 5:12; Proverbs 31:23). The wisdom tradition is particularly alert to the danger of unguarded speech: "Those who guard their mouths preserve their lives; those who open wide their lips come to ruin" (Proverbs 13:3; cf. Psalms 21:23).
4: "Do not let my heart be drawn to anything evil — to take part in the wicked acts of evildoers, or to taste any of their pleasures" — al-taṭ-libbî lĕdābār-rāʿ (do not let my heart incline to an evil thing). The prayer for divine restraint of the heart reflects the Psalter's and wisdom tradition's understanding that the heart's inclination is not fully under the individual's control — it can be "drawn" or "inclined" by external forces and internal disorders. The prayer for a guarded mouth (Psalms 141:3) and a guarded heart (Psalms 141:4) together cover the two Proverbs 4:23–27 dimensions: "Above all else guard your heart... put away perversity from your lips."
5: "If a righteous man strikes me, I will take it as a kindness; if he rebukes me, it is like fine oil on my head — I will not refuse it" — the wisdom paradox of the beneficial rebuke. "Fine oil on the head (šemen rōʾš)" — in the ANE hospitality culture, anointing a guest's head with scented oil was a signal of welcome, honor, and special status (Psalms 23:5; Luke 7:46). To accept another person's rebuke as if it were this prestigious anointing is to practice the highest wisdom disposition — treasuring correction over flattery. Proverbs returns to this theme repeatedly: "Better is open rebuke than hidden love. Faithful are the wounds of a friend; profuse are the kisses of an enemy" (Proverbs 27:5–6; cf. Psalms 9:8; Psalms 15:12; Psalms 25:12).
6: "When their leaders are hurled down from the cliffs, the people will find my words welcome" — a difficult verse. "Leaders (šōpĕṭêhem)" (their judges/rulers) hurled from cliffs may refer to the common ANE execution method for criminals and political enemies (2 Chronicles 25:12; Luke 4:29 — the crowd attempts this against Jesus at Nazareth). The sense seems to be: when the wicked rulers fall, the people will recognize that the words of the righteous speaker were true. This reflects the Psalter's eschatological confidence that vindication will come publicly.
7: "Like clods of earth turned up by the plowshare, our bones lie scattered at the brink of the grave" — kĕmô pōlēaḥ ûbōqēaʿ bāʾāreṣ niplĕgû ʿăṣāmênû. The agricultural image of broken clods of earth (from the plowing/splitting of the soil) applied to scattered bones is a visceral image of the community's extremity — scattered and exposed at the very mouth of Sheol (lĕpî šĕʾôl). The bones are the most permanent part of a person in the OT anthropology; scattered bones are the final indignity of death and defeat (Psalms 53:5; Ezekiel 37:1–14).
8: "But my eyes are fixed on you, O Lord GOD — in you I shelter; do not leave me exposed" — kî ʾēlêkā YHWH ʾadonāy ʿênāy (for to you, LORD, my Lord, are my eyes). The fixed-eyes formula expresses exclusive attention and trust directed toward YHWH rather than toward the threatening forces. "Do not leave me exposed (al-tĕʿar napšî)" — ʿārāh (to lay bare, to pour out, to leave naked and unprotected) is the word for stripping and exposing; the soul (life) left naked is utterly vulnerable. The contrast with Psalms 141:7 (bones scattered, exposed at Sheol's mouth) is stark: the soul fixes its gaze upward while the body is threatened below.
9: "Keep me from the trap they have set for me, and from the snares that evildoers have laid" — the hunting-trap imagery returns (cf. Psalms 140:5) — paḥ (trap) and môqĕšôt (snares). The repeated use of this imagery across Psalms 140–141 creates a thematic cluster: the righteous person is surrounded by hunters who have laid elaborate traps. The prayer for divine preservation from the trap is also a prayer for discernment — to see the hidden danger that the wicked have concealed in the ordinary path (Psalms 91:3: "He will save you from the fowler's snare").
10: "Let the wicked tumble into their own nets while I escape unharmed" — the retributive principle stated succinctly: the nets set for the righteous become the nets in which the wicked are caught. This is the "boomerang justice" pattern of the Psalter (Psalms 7:15–16; Psalms 9:15; Psalms 35:8; Psalms 57:6; Proverbs 26:27; Psalms 28:10). "While I escape unharmed (yaḥad ʾănî ʾeʿĕbôr)" — the simultaneous trapping of the wicked and the escape of the righteous is a single divine act. The psalm ends with confident anticipation rather than achieved deliverance — the prayer is still in progress, but its expected outcome is stated as certain.
